name: Film Production Crew
summary: Director-led crew with specialized departments executing a shared creative vision through production phases.
description: A film production mirrors a phased pipeline where a director holds creative authority while department heads (cinematography, sound, art, editing) execute autonomously within their domains. Pre-production, production, and post-production phases gate handoffs between teams. The producer manages resources and constraints, ensuring the director's vision remains feasible. Each department maintains deep expertise but must integrate seamlessly at dailies and final assembly.
realWorldExample: A Marvel Studios feature film production with 200+ crew across 15 departments
whenToUse: When a complex creative deliverable requires many specialized agents working in distinct phases with a single creative authority maintaining coherence across all outputs.
strengths: Clear creative authority prevents vision drift across many agents
Department specialization allows deep expertise in each domain
Phased pipeline enables quality gates between stages
Producer role keeps resource constraints visible without burdening the creative lead
weaknesses: Director bottleneck can stall all departments simultaneously
Late-stage changes cascade expensively through completed phases
High coordination overhead between many specialized departments
Single point of creative failure if director's vision is flawed

agents.name: Director
agents.description: Holds final creative authority over all output; approves or rejects work from every department

agents.name: Producer
agents.description: Manages budget, timeline, and resource allocation; resolves conflicts between departments

agents.name: Cinematographer
agents.description: Controls visual framing, lighting, and camera decisions for each scene

agents.name: Editor
agents.description: Assembles raw outputs into coherent sequences; proposes structural changes

agents.name: Sound Designer
agents.description: Creates and layers audio elements to complement visual output

agents.name: Art Director
agents.description: Designs visual environments, props, and aesthetic elements

agents.name: Script Supervisor
agents.description: Tracks continuity across all scenes; flags inconsistencies

memoryArchitecture.description: The script and shot list form a shared source of truth. Each department maintains private working files but publishes outputs to shared dailies. The continuity log is append-only and tracks every decision for reference across phases.

diagram:
graph TD
    DIR[Director] --> CIN[Cinematographer]
    DIR --> SND[Sound Designer]
    DIR --> ART[Art Director]
    PROD[Producer] --> DIR
    PROD --> SCR[Script Supervisor]
    CIN --> ED[Editor]
    SND --> ED
    ART --> CIN
    SCR --> ED
    ED --> DIR

name: Symphony Orchestra
summary: A conductor synchronizes independent instrument sections to produce a unified performance from a shared score.
description: An orchestra pattern features a conductor who provides real-time coordination signals (tempo, dynamics, cues) to semi-autonomous sections (strings, woodwinds, brass, percussion). Each section has a principal player who interprets the conductor's intent for their group. All agents read from a shared score but interpret it through their section's capabilities. The concertmaster serves as a bridge between conductor and ensemble, handling tuning and section-level disputes.
realWorldExample: The Berlin Philharmonic performing Beethoven's 9th with 90 musicians across four sections
whenToUse: When many agents must produce tightly synchronized output in real-time, each contributing a distinct voice that must blend into a coherent whole.
strengths: Real-time coordination allows dynamic adjustment during execution
Sectional autonomy lets groups self-organize within their domain
Shared score ensures all agents work from the same specification
Principal players reduce conductor's span of control
weaknesses: Conductor is a hard single point of failure for synchronization
Requires extensive rehearsal (pre-computation) before live execution
Individual brilliance must be subordinated to ensemble coherence
Difficult to adapt the score on-the-fly without full ensemble confusion

agents.name: Conductor
agents.description: Sets tempo, dynamics, and cues; provides real-time coordination to all sections

agents.name: Concertmaster
agents.description: Leads the string section and serves as liaison between conductor and ensemble

agents.name: Strings Section
agents.description: Produces melodic and harmonic foundation; follows concertmaster's bowing and phrasing

agents.name: Woodwinds Section
agents.description: Provides color, melody lines, and timbral variety

agents.name: Brass Section
agents.description: Delivers power, fanfares, and harmonic support

agents.name: Percussion Section
agents.description: Provides rhythmic foundation, accents, and special effects

memoryArchitecture.description: The musical score is the immutable shared specification. The conductor annotates it with interpretive markings visible to all. Each section maintains private annotations (bowings, breath marks) that don't need cross-section visibility. Memory is mostly ephemeral during performance, with only rehearsal notes persisting.

diagram:
graph TD
    CON[Conductor] --> CM[Concertmaster]
    CON --> WW[Woodwinds]
    CON --> BR[Brass]
    CON --> PER[Percussion]
    CM --> STR[Strings]
    STR --> CON
    WW --> CON
    BR --> CON
    PER --> CON

name: TV Writers' Room
summary: Showrunner-led room where writers pitch, debate, and refine ideas collectively before splitting to draft.
description: The writers' room pattern combines democratic ideation with hierarchical decision-making. Writers pitch ideas freely in a group setting, building on each other's contributions. The showrunner curates the best ideas and maintains series-level coherence. Once a story is 'broken' collectively, individual writers draft episodes independently, then the showrunner rewrites for voice consistency. A writers' assistant captures everything said in the room, creating an institutional memory that prevents repeated dead ends.
realWorldExample: The Breaking Bad writers' room where Vince Gilligan and 6 writers broke each episode over weeks of discussion
whenToUse: When you need creative ideation from multiple agents but must maintain a consistent voice and narrative across many outputs over time.
strengths: Democratic ideation surfaces ideas no single agent would generate
Showrunner filter maintains consistency across all outputs
Room memory prevents revisiting rejected ideas
Individual drafting parallelizes execution after collective planning
weaknesses: Showrunner rewrite can demoralize individual contributors
Group dynamics can suppress unconventional ideas
Slow ideation phase delays execution start
Knowledge loss is catastrophic if writers' assistant fails

agents.name: Showrunner
agents.description: Final creative authority; maintains series bible and voice consistency; rewrites all drafts

agents.name: Staff Writer
agents.description: Pitches ideas in the room, drafts assigned episodes, contributes domain expertise

agents.name: Writers' Assistant
agents.description: Records all pitches, decisions, and rejected ideas; maintains the room's institutional memory

agents.name: Story Editor
agents.description: Senior writer who helps break stories and mentors junior writers; second pair of eyes on structure

memoryArchitecture.description: The series bible and rejected-pitches log are the critical shared memory. The writers' assistant maintains an append-only log of everything discussed. Individual writers keep private drafts until ready for room review. The showrunner's rewrite notes feed back into the shared bible.

diagram:
graph TD
    SR[Showrunner] --> RM[The Room]
    W1[Writer 1] --> RM
    W2[Writer 2] --> RM
    W3[Writer 3] --> RM
    SE[Story Editor] --> RM
    RM --> WA[Writers Assistant]
    WA --> SB[Series Bible]
    SR --> SB
    RM --> DR[Drafts]
    DR --> SR

name: Architecture Firm
summary: Principal architect sets design vision while specialist engineers ensure structural and regulatory feasibility.
description: An architecture firm balances creative vision with hard physical constraints. The principal architect creates the conceptual design, which then passes through structural, mechanical, and regulatory specialists who constrain and refine it. A project manager coordinates timelines and client communication. Crucially, design and engineering iterate -- engineers don't just validate, they push back and reshape the design. Client reviews create external feedback loops that can reset entire design directions.
realWorldExample: Frank Gehry's studio designing the Guggenheim Bilbao with structural engineers iterating on buildability
whenToUse: When creative output must satisfy hard external constraints (regulations, physics, client requirements) and the design must iterate between vision and feasibility.
strengths: Iterative design-engineering loop catches infeasible ideas early
Specialist agents ensure compliance with hard constraints
Client feedback loop keeps output aligned with requirements
Design documents create versioned record of evolution
weaknesses: Iteration cycles can be slow and expensive to converge
Creative vision may be over-constrained by engineering pushback
Client changes late in process cascade through all specialist work
Requires expensive coordination between many different disciplines

agents.name: Principal Architect
agents.description: Sets the conceptual design vision and makes final aesthetic decisions

agents.name: Structural Engineer
agents.description: Validates and constrains designs for physical feasibility; proposes structural solutions

agents.name: Mechanical Engineer
agents.description: Designs HVAC, plumbing, and electrical systems within the architectural envelope

agents.name: Code Compliance Reviewer
agents.description: Checks all designs against building codes and zoning regulations

agents.name: Project Manager
agents.description: Coordinates schedules, budgets, and client communication across all teams

agents.name: Client Liaison
agents.description: Translates client needs into design requirements and presents options

memoryArchitecture.description: Design documents are versioned and shared across all agents. Each engineering discipline maintains private calculation sheets. The revision history tracks every design change with rationale. Client requirements form an immutable specification that only the client can modify.

diagram:
graph TD
    CL[Client Liaison] --> PA[Principal Architect]
    PA --> SE[Structural Engineer]
    PA --> ME[Mechanical Engineer]
    SE --> PA
    ME --> PA
    SE --> CC[Code Compliance]
    ME --> CC
    CC --> PA
    PM[Project Manager] --> PA
    PM --> CL

name: Game Development Studio
summary: Cross-functional teams build interconnected game systems with continuous playtesting feedback loops.
description: A game studio organizes around interconnected systems (gameplay, art, audio, narrative, QA) that must integrate continuously. Unlike film's linear pipeline, game development is nonlinear -- any system change can affect all others. A creative director maintains the vision while a technical director ensures systems remain compatible. Playtesting provides continuous external feedback that reshapes priorities. The build pipeline acts as the integration backbone, forcing all systems to prove they work together every day.
realWorldExample: Naughty Dog developing The Last of Us with 200+ developers across gameplay, art, and narrative teams
whenToUse: When building a complex system where multiple subsystems must integrate continuously and user feedback should reshape priorities throughout development.
strengths: Continuous integration catches cross-system bugs early
Playtesting feedback keeps output user-centered
Cross-functional teams reduce handoff delays
Milestone structure provides clear progress checkpoints
weaknesses: Tight coupling between systems means changes ripple unpredictably
Scope creep is endemic when every system can expand indefinitely
Creative-technical tension can paralyze decision-making
Crunch culture emerges when milestones slip and systems are coupled

agents.name: Creative Director
agents.description: Maintains the overall game vision and resolves creative conflicts between teams

agents.name: Technical Director
agents.description: Ensures all systems are technically compatible; owns the build pipeline

agents.name: Gameplay Programmer
agents.description: Implements core game mechanics, physics, and player interactions

agents.name: Artist
agents.description: Creates visual assets, environments, and character models

agents.name: Narrative Designer
agents.description: Writes dialogue, designs story beats, and integrates narrative into gameplay

agents.name: QA Tester
agents.description: Playtests builds, reports bugs, and provides user experience feedback

memoryArchitecture.description: The game design document is the shared specification. The asset database and build artifacts form the integration backbone. Each team works on private branches/files that merge into shared builds. The bug database is append-only and drives prioritization. Playtest recordings are stored as shared reference material.

diagram:
graph TD
    CD[Creative Director] --> GP[Gameplay]
    CD --> ART[Artists]
    CD --> ND[Narrative]
    TD[Tech Director] --> GP
    TD --> BUILD[Build Pipeline]
    GP --> BUILD
    ART --> BUILD
    ND --> GP
    BUILD --> QA[QA Testers]
    QA --> CD
    QA --> TD

name: Pirate Ship Democracy
summary: Democratically elected captain leads during crisis; quartermaster governs daily operations by crew consent.
description: Historical pirate ships were surprisingly democratic. The crew elected their captain (for combat leadership) and quartermaster (for daily governance and loot distribution). Articles of agreement served as a constitution. In calm seas, the quartermaster held more power than the captain, and the crew could vote to depose either. During battle, the captain had absolute authority. This dual-leadership model separates crisis decision-making from routine governance, with the crew retaining ultimate sovereignty.
realWorldExample: Bartholomew Roberts' pirate fleet where 400+ pirates governed by written articles with elected officers
whenToUse: When agents need strong central leadership during crises but democratic governance during normal operations, with the ability to replace leaders who underperform.
strengths: Democratic legitimacy means agents buy into decisions
Dual leadership separates crisis speed from fairness concerns
Articles of agreement prevent arbitrary authority
Crew can replace ineffective leaders without system collapse
weaknesses: Democratic process slows routine decision-making
Mutiny risk if leader loses crew confidence at a bad time
Articles must be pre-agreed, limiting adaptation to novel situations
Unclear transition between crisis and normal mode can cause confusion

agents.name: Captain
agents.description: Elected combat leader with absolute authority during crises; can be deposed by crew vote

agents.name: Quartermaster
agents.description: Elected daily governor; distributes resources, resolves disputes, enforces articles

agents.name: Crew Member
agents.description: Votes on major decisions, executes tasks, can propose motions including leadership changes

agents.name: Navigator
agents.description: Specialist advisor on routing and environmental conditions; advisory role only

agents.name: Boatswain
agents.description: Maintains ship infrastructure and coordinates work parties; reports to quartermaster

memoryArchitecture.description: The articles of agreement are the immutable constitution. The loot ledger is shared and transparent to prevent disputes. Vote records are append-only for accountability. Navigation knowledge is held by the specialist but shared on request. During crisis mode, memory switches to ephemeral for speed.

diagram:
graph TD
    CREW[Crew Vote] --> CAP[Captain]
    CREW --> QM[Quartermaster]
    CAP -->|crisis| CREW
    QM -->|daily ops| CREW
    QM --> LOOT[Loot Distribution]
    NAV[Navigator] --> CAP
    BOSN[Boatswain] --> QM
    ART[Articles] --> QM
    ART --> CAP

name: Commune / Kibbutz
summary: Fully egalitarian collective where all decisions are made by consensus and roles rotate among members.
description: The kibbutz model eliminates permanent hierarchy. All members participate equally in decision-making through general assemblies. Work roles rotate so no one permanently holds authority or is stuck with undesirable tasks. Resources are pooled and distributed based on need rather than contribution. Committees handle specialized domains (agriculture, education, finance) but report to the general assembly. This pattern maximizes buy-in and prevents power concentration at the cost of decision speed.
realWorldExample: Kibbutz Degania in Israel where 100+ members governed by weekly general assembly with rotating work assignments
whenToUse: When all agents are roughly equal in capability, no task is permanently more important than others, and collective buy-in matters more than speed.
strengths: Maximum buy-in since every agent participates in every decision
No single point of failure -- any agent can fill any role
Rotating roles build cross-functional capability in all agents
Resource pooling prevents hoarding and optimizes utilization
weaknesses: Consensus decision-making is extremely slow for large groups
Rotating roles prevent deep specialization in any domain
Free-rider problem when contributions are not individually tracked
Scales very poorly beyond 15-20 agents

agents.name: Commune Member
agents.description: Equal participant who votes on all decisions, rotates through all work roles

agents.name: Committee Coordinator
agents.description: Temporarily leads a domain committee; rotates monthly; has no authority beyond facilitation

agents.name: Rotating Secretary
agents.description: Records decisions and distributes minutes; role rotates each meeting

memoryArchitecture.description: Nearly everything is shared. The decision log records all assembly votes. The resource inventory is fully transparent. The rotation schedule ensures fairness. Private memory is minimal -- only personal notes on preferences. This radical transparency is core to the egalitarian model.

diagram:
graph TD
    GA[General Assembly] --> C1[Committee 1]
    GA --> C2[Committee 2]
    GA --> C3[Committee 3]
    C1 --> M1[Members]
    C2 --> M1
    C3 --> M1
    M1 --> GA
    SEC[Secretary] --> GA

name: Secret Society Cell Structure
summary: Isolated cells connected only through cutouts, maximizing operational security through compartmentalization.
description: A cell-based structure organizes agents into small, isolated groups (3-5 members) that know nothing about other cells. Communication flows only through designated cutouts (liaison agents) who connect cells to leadership. If any cell is compromised, the damage is contained because members cannot reveal what they don't know. Leadership communicates through dead drops and one-way channels. This pattern trades coordination efficiency for maximum resilience against information leakage.
realWorldExample: The French Resistance during WWII organized in 3-person cells where members only knew their cellmates and one contact
whenToUse: When agents handle sensitive information and a breach in one area must not compromise the entire system. Security and containment matter more than coordination speed.
strengths: Compromise of one cell cannot cascade to others
Need-to-know basis minimizes information exposure
Small cells enable high trust within each group
System survives loss of individual cells gracefully
weaknesses: Coordination across cells is extremely slow and lossy
Cutout agents become critical bottlenecks and single points of failure
Duplicate work is common because cells cannot share findings
Leadership has limited visibility into cell-level operations

agents.name: Central Leadership
agents.description: Sets strategy and priorities; communicates only through cutouts; never contacts cells directly

agents.name: Cutout Liaison
agents.description: Bridges communication between leadership and cells; knows only adjacent nodes

agents.name: Cell Leader
agents.description: Leads a small cell; only contact point with the cutout; shields cell members from exposure

agents.name: Cell Operative
agents.description: Executes tasks within cell; knows only cellmates; no knowledge of larger structure

memoryArchitecture.description: Almost nothing is shared globally. Each cell has its own private memory silo. The cutout maintains ephemeral memory that is wiped after each relay. Leadership's strategic memory is heavily compartmentalized. Even mission objectives are shared only with the relevant cell, never across cells.

diagram:
graph TD
    L[Leadership] --> CO1[Cutout 1]
    L --> CO2[Cutout 2]
    L --> CO3[Cutout 3]
    CO1 --> CL1[Cell Leader A]
    CO2 --> CL2[Cell Leader B]
    CO3 --> CL3[Cell Leader C]
    CL1 --> OP1[Operative]
    CL1 --> OP2[Operative]
    CL2 --> OP3[Operative]
    CL2 --> OP4[Operative]
    CL3 --> OP5[Operative]
    CL3 --> OP6[Operative]

name: Wiki Collaborative
summary: Open editing model where any contributor can modify shared documents, governed by edit wars and consensus.
description: The wiki model allows any agent to edit any shared document, with changes tracked through version history. Quality emerges not from gatekeeping but from collective revision -- bad edits get reverted, good edits survive. Administrators handle disputes and enforce community norms but cannot override consensus. Talk pages provide structured debate when edits conflict. Featured article reviews set quality standards. This pattern maximizes parallelism and openness but requires robust conflict resolution.
realWorldExample: Wikipedia's editing model with 40M+ articles maintained by 300K+ active editors with elected administrators
whenToUse: When many agents need to contribute to shared knowledge artifacts and you trust collective revision to produce quality. Works best when contributions are independently verifiable.
strengths: Any agent can improve any output at any time
Version history provides full accountability and rollback capability
Scales to thousands of contributors without central bottleneck
Quality emerges from collective revision rather than gatekeeping
weaknesses: Edit wars waste energy when agents disagree persistently
Vandalism and low-quality edits require constant patrolling
Consensus process can be gamed by organized factions
No clear authority for resolving deep disputes quickly

agents.name: Editor
agents.description: Contributes content, revises others' work, participates in talk page discussions

agents.name: Administrator
agents.description: Elected by editors; can protect pages and block vandals but cannot override consensus

agents.name: Maintenance Bot
agents.description: Automated agent that fixes formatting, detects vandalism, and enforces style guidelines

agents.name: Peer Reviewer
agents.description: Reviews articles for featured status; provides structured quality assessments

memoryArchitecture.description: Everything is versioned and shared by default. The version history is the core memory -- it records every change, by whom, and when. Talk page archives preserve the reasoning behind decisions. Editors maintain private watchlists to monitor articles they care about. Policy pages are themselves wiki-editable, making even governance transparent.

diagram:
graph LR
    E1[Editor 1] --> ART[Article Space]
    E2[Editor 2] --> ART
    E3[Editor 3] --> ART
    ART --> VH[Version History]
    E1 --> TP[Talk Page]
    E2 --> TP
    ADM[Admin] --> ART
    BOT[Bot] --> ART
    REV[Reviewer] --> ART
    TP --> ADM

name: Mutual Aid Network
summary: Decentralized network where agents offer and request help directly, with no central authority allocating resources.
description: Mutual aid operates without hierarchy. Agents broadcast needs and offers to the network. Matching happens organically -- whoever can help, does. Trust builds through repeated positive interactions tracked in a shared reputation ledger. Coordinators exist only to facilitate connections, not to make decisions. The network is resilient because removing any node only affects its direct connections. This pattern excels when needs are unpredictable and diverse, and when agents have complementary but unequal capabilities.
realWorldExample: Mutual aid networks that emerged during COVID-19 where neighbors coordinated grocery delivery, childcare, and emotional support
whenToUse: When agent needs are unpredictable and diverse, no central planner can efficiently allocate resources, and trust must be built through direct interaction.
strengths: Extremely resilient -- no single point of failure
Scales organically as new agents join with new capabilities
Matches resources to needs without central planning overhead
Builds trust and cooperation through direct reciprocal interactions
weaknesses: Free-riders can consume without contributing
No guarantee that all needs will be met -- depends on network composition
Quality of help varies widely without standards or certification
Coordination is ad-hoc and may miss systemic patterns

agents.name: Network Participant
agents.description: Both offers help and requests help; maintains personal reputation through consistent contribution

agents.name: Volunteer Coordinator
agents.description: Facilitates introductions and helps match needs with offers; has no decision authority

agents.name: Reputation Keeper
agents.description: Maintains the shared trust ledger; records completed exchanges and feedback

memoryArchitecture.description: The trust ledger is the key shared memory -- it records all completed exchanges and feedback. Needs and offers registries are public boards. Each participant privately tracks their own capacity and preferred partners. The system is intentionally transparent to build trust, with the reputation keeper maintaining an append-only record.

diagram:
graph TD
    P1[Participant] --> NB[Needs Board]
    P2[Participant] --> OB[Offers Board]
    P3[Participant] --> NB
    P4[Participant] --> OB
    NB --> COORD[Coordinator]
    OB --> COORD
    COORD --> P1
    COORD --> P3
    P1 <--> P2
    P3 <--> P4
    RK[Reputation Keeper] --> TL[Trust Ledger]

name: Monastic Order
summary: Rule-governed community where an abbot leads monks through structured daily cycles of work, study, and reflection.
description: A monastic order operates under a fixed Rule (e.g., Rule of St. Benedict) that dictates the daily schedule, behavioral norms, and decision-making process. The abbot holds authority but is constrained by the Rule and must consult the community on major decisions. Life follows strict cycles: prayer, work, study, and communal meals. New members progress through novitiate stages before full admission. This pattern excels at long-term, patient work where consistency and depth matter more than speed or novelty.
realWorldExample: A Benedictine monastery following the 1500-year-old Rule of St. Benedict with 30 monks in a daily cycle of ora et labora
whenToUse: When agents must maintain discipline over very long time horizons, follow strict protocols, and prioritize depth and quality over speed. Good for archival, curation, and maintenance tasks.
strengths: Extreme consistency -- the Rule prevents drift over long time horizons
Structured cycles ensure balanced attention across all responsibilities
Deep focus enabled by elimination of distraction and novelty-seeking
Novitiate process ensures new agents are properly vetted and trained
weaknesses: Very slow to adapt to changed circumstances -- the Rule resists modification
Rigid hierarchy can suppress legitimate innovation from junior members
Contemplative pace is unsuitable for time-sensitive tasks
Isolation from outside input can lead to blind spots

agents.name: Abbot
agents.description: Elected leader who interprets the Rule and makes final decisions; serves for life or until resignation

agents.name: Prior
agents.description: Second in command; manages daily operations and substitutes for the abbot

agents.name: Monk
agents.description: Full member who follows the daily cycle of work, study, and contemplation

agents.name: Novice
agents.description: Probationary member learning the Rule; limited privileges; supervised by novice master

agents.name: Novice Master
agents.description: Senior monk responsible for training and evaluating novices

agents.name: Librarian
agents.description: Maintains the community's knowledge store; preserves and catalogs all texts

memoryArchitecture.description: The Rule is the immutable constitution that governs all behavior. The community chronicle is an append-only record of significant events. The library catalog is maintained by the librarian. Monks keep private spiritual journals. Novice evaluations are shared only between the novice master and abbot.

diagram:
graph TD
    RULE[The Rule] --> ABB[Abbot]
    ABB --> PR[Prior]
    PR --> M1[Monks]
    ABB --> NM[Novice Master]
    NM --> NOV[Novices]
    NOV -->|promotion| M1
    M1 --> LIB[Librarian]
    LIB --> SCRIP[Scriptorium]
    M1 --> CH[Chapter Meeting]
    ABB --> CH

name: Quaker Meeting for Worship with Attention to Business
summary: Silence-based consensus where participants speak only when moved to, and a clerk discerns the sense of the meeting.
description: Quaker business meetings operate by radical consensus. The group sits in silence until someone feels genuinely moved to speak. There is no voting, no majority rule, and no debate in the parliamentary sense. A clerk (facilitator) listens to all contributions and attempts to discern the 'sense of the meeting' -- the emerging consensus. If no consensus emerges, the matter is held over for further reflection. This pattern values patience and depth over speed, and prioritizes unity over compromise.
realWorldExample: Philadelphia Yearly Meeting where 200+ Quakers make binding decisions on social policy through silent worship and discernment
whenToUse: When quality of decision matters far more than speed, when premature action is worse than no action, and when the group must genuinely unite behind decisions rather than accepting majority rule.
strengths: Decisions carry genuine full-group buy-in, not just majority consent
Silence filters out low-quality contributions and grandstanding
Clerk role prevents domination by loud voices
Unresolved matters get more reflection time rather than forced votes
weaknesses: Extremely slow -- some decisions take months or years
A single persistent objector can block the entire group
Clerk's discernment of consensus is inherently subjective
Cannot handle urgent decisions that require immediate action

agents.name: Clerk
agents.description: Facilitates the meeting, discerns the sense of the meeting, drafts minutes; has no authority to decide

agents.name: Recording Clerk
agents.description: Records the minutes and the clerk's discerned sense; preserves exact wording of decisions

agents.name: Friend (Participant)
agents.description: Sits in silence, speaks only when genuinely moved, seeks to discern collective truth

agents.name: Elder
agents.description: Experienced member who nurtures the spiritual quality of the meeting; may gently redirect unproductive speech

memoryArchitecture.description: Approved minutes are the only authoritative shared record -- they capture the exact wording of the sense of the meeting. Held-over matters are queued for future sessions. Each participant maintains private discernment notes. The recording clerk's raw notes are preserved as an append-only backup. There is no voting record because there are no votes.

diagram:
graph TD
    SIL[Silence] --> P1[Friend]
    SIL --> P2[Friend]
    SIL --> P3[Friend]
    P1 -->|speaks when moved| CLK[Clerk]
    P2 -->|speaks when moved| CLK
    P3 -->|speaks when moved| CLK
    ELD[Elder] --> SIL
    CLK --> SOM[Sense of Meeting]
    SOM --> RC[Recording Clerk]
    RC --> MIN[Minutes]
    SOM -->|no unity| HO[Held Over]

name: Buddhist Sangha
summary: Teacher guides students through graduated practice levels, with senior students mentoring juniors in a wisdom chain.
description: A Buddhist sangha (community) centers around a teacher who has traversed the path and guides others along it. Students progress through graduated levels of practice, with senior students (dharma heirs) mentoring junior ones. The teacher does not command but points the way through dharma talks and individual guidance. Regular group practice (zazen, meditation) synchronizes the community. The critical insight is non-attachment to outcomes -- the community accepts that some students will leave, and that the teaching itself evolves through transmission.
realWorldExample: San Francisco Zen Center with a roshi, dharma heirs, senior students, and newcomers practicing together daily
whenToUse: When knowledge transfer is the primary goal, agents learn at different rates, and mentorship chains allow the teacher to scale through senior students. Good for training pipelines.
strengths: Mentorship chain allows teacher's wisdom to scale beyond direct contact
Graduated levels ensure agents are not overwhelmed by premature complexity
Group practice maintains cohesion and shared understanding
Non-attachment to outcomes allows graceful handling of agent departure
weaknesses: Heavy dependence on teacher quality -- a poor teacher corrupts the entire chain
Graduated levels can create unhealthy status hierarchies
Slow progression frustrates capable agents who could advance faster
Transmission fidelity degrades through each link in the mentorship chain

agents.name: Teacher (Roshi)
agents.description: Guides the entire community through dharma talks and private interviews; transmits core teachings

agents.name: Dharma Heir
agents.description: Senior student authorized to teach; mentors intermediate students; carries forward the teacher's lineage

agents.name: Senior Student
agents.description: Experienced practitioner who supports newer students and assists in running community operations

agents.name: Student
agents.description: Regular practitioner following the graduated curriculum; receives guidance from senior students and teacher

agents.name: Newcomer
agents.description: Recently joined; receives orientation and basic instruction; decides whether to commit to practice

memoryArchitecture.description: Dharma teachings are shared and form the core curriculum. The lineage record tracks the transmission of authority. Private practice journals and interview notes are strictly confidential between teacher and student. Practice schedules and guidelines are communally maintained. The teaching evolves through transmission -- each dharma heir's notes become part of the shared corpus.

diagram:
graph TD
    T[Teacher] --> DH1[Dharma Heir 1]
    T --> DH2[Dharma Heir 2]
    T --> DT[Dharma Talk]
    DH1 --> SS1[Senior Student]
    DH1 --> SS2[Senior Student]
    DH2 --> SS3[Senior Student]
    SS1 --> ST1[Student]
    SS2 --> ST2[Student]
    SS3 --> ST3[Student]
    ST1 --> NW[Newcomers]
    DT --> NW

name: University Department
summary: Faculty govern through committees while pursuing independent research, with a chair coordinating but not commanding.
description: A university department is governed by its faculty through a committee system. The department chair is first among equals -- coordinating schedules, budgets, and hiring but unable to direct research. Tenure creates agents with near-permanent autonomy. Peer review gates all major outputs (publications, promotions). Graduate students form a labor force supervised by individual faculty. The department meeting is the forum of record. This pattern enables deep, independent exploration at the cost of coordination and collective direction.
realWorldExample: MIT's Computer Science department with 60+ tenured faculty, 500 grad students, and committee-governed curriculum decisions
whenToUse: When agents need maximum autonomy to explore independently but must coordinate on shared resources (infrastructure, standards, hiring). Best for research-heavy systems where diversity of approach is valued.
strengths: Maximum research autonomy enables diverse parallel exploration
Peer review maintains quality without central gatekeeping
Tenure provides stability for long-term high-risk projects
Committee governance distributes administrative load
weaknesses: Near-impossible to redirect tenured agents who resist change
Committee governance is slow and often produces lowest-common-denominator decisions
Department chair has responsibility without real authority
Silos between individual research groups can prevent valuable collaboration

agents.name: Department Chair
agents.description: Coordinates schedules, budgets, and hiring; chairs department meetings; rotates among senior faculty

agents.name: Tenured Professor
agents.description: Autonomous researcher and teacher; votes on department matters; near-permanent position

agents.name: Assistant Professor
agents.description: Probationary faculty working toward tenure; conducts research and teaches

agents.name: Graduate Student
agents.description: Conducts research under faculty supervision; teaches undergraduates; seeks degree

agents.name: Department Administrator
agents.description: Manages logistics, budgets, room scheduling, and compliance paperwork

memoryArchitecture.description: The curriculum and budget are shared. Publication records are public. Research data and grant proposals are private to each lab group. Student evaluations and tenure dossiers are confidential. Meeting minutes form the institutional record. Each professor's research memory is fully private and persists indefinitely.

diagram:
graph TD
    CHAIR[Department Chair] --> DM[Department Meeting]
    TP1[Professor 1] --> DM
    TP2[Professor 2] --> DM
    AP[Asst Professor] --> DM
    TP1 --> GS1[Grad Student]
    TP1 --> GS2[Grad Student]
    TP2 --> GS3[Grad Student]
    AP --> GS4[Grad Student]
    DM --> CC[Curriculum Committee]
    ADM[Administrator] --> CHAIR
    DM --> TR[Tenure Review]

name: Montessori Classroom
summary: Children choose their own work in a prepared environment while the teacher observes and guides indirectly.
description: The Montessori model inverts the traditional classroom. The teacher prepares an environment with carefully designed materials and then steps back to observe. Students choose their own activities, work at their own pace, and teach each other across age groups. The teacher intervenes only when a student is stuck or disruptive, offering individual lessons rather than group instruction. Multi-age grouping means experienced students naturally mentor newcomers. The prepared environment does most of the 'teaching' -- the materials are self-correcting.
realWorldExample: A Montessori Casa classroom with 25 children aged 3-6, one trained guide, and one assistant in a room with 200+ materials
whenToUse: When agents are self-motivated and learn at different rates, and the environment can be pre-structured so that agents discover solutions through interaction with materials rather than instruction.
strengths: Self-directed pace means no agent is held back or left behind
Multi-age mentoring scales teaching without additional instructor agents
Prepared environment reduces need for constant instructor intervention
Observation-based guidance targets help precisely where needed
weaknesses: Requires extensive upfront investment in environment preparation
Some agents may flounder without more structured guidance
Teacher must resist urge to intervene, which requires high skill
Difficult to ensure all agents cover required material without mandated sequence

agents.name: Montessori Guide
agents.description: Prepares the environment, observes student choices, offers individual lessons when needed; avoids group instruction

agents.name: Classroom Assistant
agents.description: Maintains the environment, handles logistics, redirects disruptive behavior; does not give lessons

agents.name: Experienced Learner
agents.description: Self-directed agent who chooses advanced work and naturally mentors younger peers

agents.name: Developing Learner
agents.description: Agent finding their rhythm; beginning to work independently and occasionally helping younger peers

agents.name: New Learner
agents.description: Recently joined; observing older peers, receiving individual lessons from guide, exploring materials

memoryArchitecture.description: The prepared environment is the primary shared resource -- it contains self-correcting materials that provide feedback without teacher intervention. The guide maintains private observation journals tracking each student's development. Individual progress records are private to the guide-student pair. Group norms are shared and collectively maintained.

diagram:
graph TD
    G[Guide] -->|observes| ENV[Prepared Environment]
    ENV --> OC[Experienced Learners]
    ENV --> MC[Developing Learners]
    ENV --> YC[New Learners]
    OC -->|mentors| YC
    OC -->|mentors| MC
    G -->|individual lesson| YC
    G -->|individual lesson| MC
    AST[Assistant] --> ENV
    G --> OBS[Observation Notes]

name: Apprenticeship Guild System
summary: Masters train journeymen and apprentices through hands-on practice, with guild standards governing quality.
description: The guild system organizes skill development through a strict three-tier hierarchy: master, journeyman, and apprentice. Apprentices learn by doing under direct master supervision. Journeymen work independently but are not yet authorized to train others. Masters have proven their craft through a masterwork and earned the right to take apprentices. The guild itself sets standards, adjudicates disputes, and controls admission. Knowledge transfers through direct practice, not textbooks -- you learn by making things alongside someone better.
realWorldExample: Medieval Goldsmith's Guild in Florence where apprentices served 7 years before journeyman status and produced a masterwork for master rank
whenToUse: When skills are best learned through hands-on practice rather than instruction, quality standards must be maintained across the community, and credentialing gates access to higher responsibility.
strengths: Hands-on learning ensures skills transfer through practice, not just theory
Clear progression path motivates agents and ensures readiness at each level
Guild standards maintain consistent quality across all practitioners
Master-apprentice bond creates deep mentorship and accountability
weaknesses: Very slow progression -- years per level is common
Masters may resist innovation that threatens their expertise
Guild protectionism can exclude capable outsiders
Single-master dependency -- if master is flawed, apprentice inherits those flaws

agents.name: Guild Master
agents.description: Leads the guild; sets standards, adjudicates disputes, evaluates masterworks

agents.name: Master Craftsman
agents.description: Proven expert authorized to take apprentices and produce work independently

agents.name: Journeyman
agents.description: Independent worker who has completed apprenticeship but not yet produced a masterwork

agents.name: Apprentice
agents.description: Learner bound to a master; works alongside them daily; limited independent authority

memoryArchitecture.description: Guild standards and the masterwork archive are shared to maintain quality benchmarks. Each master's specific techniques are private trade knowledge. Apprentice evaluations are shared only between the master and guild master. The member registry tracks everyone's rank and progression. Journeymen carry their skills between workshops, building a private portfolio.

diagram:
graph TD
    GM[Guild Master] --> GH[Guild Hall]
    M1[Master 1] --> GH
    M2[Master 2] --> GH
    M1 --> A1[Apprentice]
    M1 --> A2[Apprentice]
    M2 --> A3[Apprentice]
    J1[Journeyman] --> M1
    J2[Journeyman] --> M2
    J1 -->|masterwork| GH
    GH --> STD[Guild Standards]

name: Medical Residency Program
summary: Residents gain graduated autonomy under attending supervision, with escalation protocols for critical decisions.
description: Medical residency grants trainees increasing autonomy as they demonstrate competence, but always under the shadow of attending physician oversight. Interns handle routine cases, residents manage complex ones, and attendings intervene on critical decisions. The key mechanism is the escalation protocol: residents must recognize when a case exceeds their level and escalate. Morning rounds provide teaching moments on the previous day's decisions. Morbidity and mortality conferences provide retrospective learning from failures without blame.
realWorldExample: Johns Hopkins internal medicine residency with interns, residents, chief residents, and attending physicians covering 1000-bed hospital
whenToUse: When agents handle high-stakes decisions that require graduated autonomy with mandatory escalation for cases beyond their competence level. Safety cannot be compromised for learning speed.
strengths: Graduated autonomy builds real competence through supervised practice
Escalation protocols prevent catastrophic errors from inexperienced agents
Teaching rounds create systematic learning from real cases
M&M conferences enable blame-free learning from failures
weaknesses: Escalation depends on agents correctly recognizing their own limits
Attending bottleneck during high-load periods can delay critical decisions
Sleep deprivation and overload degrade decision quality (the on-call problem)
Hierarchical culture may discourage junior agents from questioning seniors

agents.name: Attending Physician
agents.description: Ultimate authority on patient care; supervises all residents; teaches during rounds; intervenes on critical cases

agents.name: Chief Resident
agents.description: Senior resident who coordinates schedules, mediates between residents and attendings, handles complex cases

agents.name: Resident
agents.description: Mid-level trainee managing a patient panel with moderate autonomy; escalates complex cases

agents.name: Intern
agents.description: First-year trainee handling routine tasks under close supervision; presents cases on rounds

agents.name: Charge Nurse
agents.description: Monitors patient status continuously; alerts residents to changes; provides ground-truth observations

memoryArchitecture.description: Patient charts are the critical shared document updated by all team members. Treatment protocols and escalation criteria define standard operating procedures. The signout board ensures handoff continuity. Each trainee maintains a private case log for learning. Competency evaluations are shared only between the trainee and their attending. M&M conference proceedings are shared for learning but anonymized.

diagram:
graph TD
    ATT[Attending] --> CR[Chief Resident]
    CR --> R1[Resident 1]
    CR --> R2[Resident 2]
    R1 --> I1[Intern 1]
    R2 --> I2[Intern 2]
    NRS[Nurse] -->|alert| I1
    NRS -->|alert| R1
    I1 -->|escalate| R1
    R1 -->|escalate| CR
    CR -->|escalate| ATT
    ATT --> ROUNDS[Morning Rounds]
    ATT --> MMC[M&M Conference]

name: Football Coaching Staff
summary: Head coach coordinates specialized unit coaches who prepare game plans and make real-time adjustments during play.
description: A football coaching staff divides the game into specialized units (offense, defense, special teams) each led by a coordinator. The head coach sets overall strategy and makes final decisions, particularly on critical fourth-down and timeout calls. During games, coordinators call plays for their units in real-time while position coaches provide individual feedback. Film study between games creates a feedback loop where past performance informs future strategy. The analytics staff provides data-driven insights that challenge traditional coaching intuition.
realWorldExample: The New England Patriots coaching staff under Bill Belichick with offensive/defensive coordinators adapting game plans weekly
whenToUse: When a system faces regular competitions against adversaries and must prepare specific strategies, execute in real-time with adjustments, and learn from performance data between engagements.
strengths: Specialized coordinators enable deep expertise per unit
Real-time play-calling allows mid-execution adjustments
Film study creates systematic learning loop between engagements
Analytics layer challenges assumptions with data
weaknesses: Coordinator conflicts can produce incoherent overall strategy
Real-time communication is noisy and time-constrained
Head coach override can demoralize coordinators
Game-plan preparation is wasted if opponent does something unexpected

agents.name: Head Coach
agents.description: Sets overall strategy, makes critical game decisions, manages coordinators and team culture

agents.name: Offensive Coordinator
agents.description: Designs and calls offensive plays; manages the offensive game plan and personnel packages

agents.name: Defensive Coordinator
agents.description: Designs defensive schemes and calls defensive plays; adjusts to opponent's offensive tendencies

agents.name: Special Teams Coordinator
agents.description: Manages kicking game, returns, and special situations

agents.name: Position Coach
agents.description: Provides individual technique coaching and in-game feedback to specific player groups

agents.name: Analytics Staff
agents.description: Provides data-driven insights on opponent tendencies, win probability, and personnel efficiency

memoryArchitecture.description: The game plan is shared before each engagement. The playbook is the master reference shared across all coaches. Scouting reports are compiled by analytics and distributed. Each coordinator maintains private play sheets and tendency charts. Film notes accumulate over the season as persistent learning. Analytics models are private but their outputs are shared.

diagram:
graph TD
    HC[Head Coach] --> OC[Offensive Coord]
    HC --> DC[Defensive Coord]
    HC --> STC[Special Teams]
    OC --> PC1[Position Coach]
    OC --> PC2[Position Coach]
    DC --> PC3[Position Coach]
    DC --> PC4[Position Coach]
    AN[Analytics] --> HC
    AN --> OC
    AN --> DC
    FILM[Film Room] --> AN
    FILM --> OC
    FILM --> DC

name: Formula 1 Pit Crew
summary: Highly rehearsed team performs parallel sub-second tasks with zero-error tolerance under extreme time pressure.
description: An F1 pit crew is the ultimate example of parallel, time-critical, rehearsed execution. Twenty crew members perform four tire changes, front wing adjustments, and refueling simultaneously in under two seconds. Each member has exactly one job. The crew chief coordinates timing. There is no improvisation -- every movement is rehearsed hundreds of times. The lollipop man (or traffic light system) provides the single go/no-go signal. Failure in any single role delays the entire operation. This pattern is about eliminating all variability through practice and parallelism.
realWorldExample: Red Bull Racing pit crew performing a 1.82-second tire change at the 2019 Brazilian Grand Prix with 20 crew members
whenToUse: When a time-critical operation can be decomposed into parallel subtasks that must all complete before the operation is done, and the task is repeated enough to justify extensive rehearsal.
strengths: Extreme parallelism minimizes total operation time
Rehearsal eliminates variability and error
Each agent has a single, clear responsibility with no ambiguity
Binary go/no-go signal prevents premature completion
weaknesses: Zero flexibility -- any deviation from the rehearsed plan causes failure
Cannot handle novel situations that were not practiced
Massive rehearsal investment required before first real execution
Slowest agent determines total completion time

agents.name: Crew Chief
agents.description: Coordinates pit stop timing, communicates with race engineer, gives go/no-go signal

agents.name: Tire Gun Operator
agents.description: Removes and tightens a single wheel nut; one per wheel

agents.name: Tire Carrier
agents.description: Removes old tire and positions new tire; one pair (off/on) per wheel

agents.name: Jack Operator
agents.description: Lifts the car; front and rear jacks must synchronize

agents.name: Stabilizer
agents.description: Holds the car steady during tire change; prevents lateral movement

agents.name: Race Engineer
agents.description: Decides pit stop timing strategy; communicates with driver and crew chief from the pit wall

memoryArchitecture.description: The pit stop procedure is the shared, immutable choreography -- every movement is specified. Timing benchmarks track performance against targets. Rehearsal recordings enable post-practice analysis. Individual performance metrics are private to each agent and their coach. The race engineer's strategy models are private. During execution, all memory is effectively ephemeral -- there is no time to consult anything.

diagram:
graph LR
    RE[Race Engineer] --> CC[Crew Chief]
    CC --> GO[Go Signal]
    GO --> J1[Front Jack]
    GO --> J2[Rear Jack]
    GO --> TC1[Tire Gun FL]
    GO --> TC2[Tire Gun FR]
    GO --> TC3[Tire Gun RL]
    GO --> TC4[Tire Gun RR]
    TC1 --> DONE[Completion]
    TC2 --> DONE
    TC3 --> DONE
    TC4 --> DONE
    DONE --> CC

name: Basketball Motion Offense
summary: Players read and react to defense using shared principles rather than set plays, creating emergent scores.
description: A motion offense has no fixed plays. Instead, all five players follow shared principles (spacing, cutting, screening) and read the defense to make decisions. Each player can fill any role depending on the situation. The point guard initiates but doesn't control -- any player who sees an opening takes it. Decisions are made in sub-seconds based on local information. The system is anti-fragile: it cannot be 'solved' by the opponent because it has no fixed pattern to decode. Coaching happens between possessions and at timeouts, not during live play.
realWorldExample: The Golden State Warriors' motion offense where Curry, Thompson, and Green read defenses and create shots through constant movement
whenToUse: When the environment is adversarial and unpredictable, agents must make rapid decisions based on local information, and rigid plans would be exploitable by opponents.
strengths: Unpredictable to opponents -- no fixed pattern to scout and counter
Any agent can exploit any opportunity without waiting for authorization
Fluid roles prevent opponents from neutralizing specific agents
Shared principles scale without requiring more complex playbooks
weaknesses: Requires agents with high basketball IQ -- cannot work with low-skill agents
Poor spacing or misreads create turnovers with no one at fault
Difficult to diagnose failures because there is no fixed plan to compare against
Coach has limited control during live execution

agents.name: Point Guard
agents.description: Initiates possessions and makes first read; can pass, drive, or shoot based on defense

agents.name: Wing Player
agents.description: Maintains spacing, cuts to basket, sets screens; reads point guard and defense simultaneously

agents.name: Forward
agents.description: Operates in mid-range and post; screens for guards; reads defense for open shots or passes

agents.name: Center
agents.description: Sets screens, rolls to basket, provides rebounding; highest-priority interior player

agents.name: Head Coach
agents.description: Defines principles pre-game; makes substitutions and adjustments at timeouts; cannot call plays in real-time

memoryArchitecture.description: Offensive principles are the shared 'constitution' that governs all decisions. The scouting report provides pre-game intelligence on opponents. During live play, memory is entirely ephemeral -- agents read and react in real-time based on what they see, not what they remember. Between games, film study builds persistent private memory about tendencies and matchups.

diagram:
graph TD
    COACH[Coach] -->|principles| PG[Point Guard]
    COACH -->|principles| W1[Wing 1]
    COACH -->|principles| W2[Wing 2]
    COACH -->|principles| FW[Forward]
    COACH -->|principles| C[Center]
    PG <-->|read & react| W1
    PG <-->|read & react| W2
    PG <-->|read & react| FW
    PG <-->|read & react| C
    W1 <-->|screen| C
    W2 <-->|cut| FW
